speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start with your vitals — your diastolic blood pressure today is 79 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: Your body temperature is normal at 37.1 °C.

speaker: patient
text: Thanks, doctor. I’ve been feeling a bit off lately — tired all the time, even after a full night’s sleep.

speaker: doctor
text: I see. Fatigue can have many causes — we’ll explore that. Let’s check your heart rate next.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Metformin 500mg twice daily for blood sugar control.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose this morning was 8.3 mmol/L — slightly elevated but not alarming yet.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve had fatigue for about two weeks now — it’s worse in the afternoons.

speaker: doctor
text: Any chest discomfort, shortness of breath, or dizziness with activity?

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 98 bpm — a bit elevated but within acceptable range for now.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history, stress test results, and angiogram, your primary diagnosis remains Coronary Artery Disease.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m going to switch you from Atorvastatin 20mg to 40mg once daily at bedtime to better manage your LDL and reduce cardiovascular risk.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that tonight. Should I monitor anything specific?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — watch for muscle aches or dark urine, and continue checking your blood sugar regularly.

speaker: doctor
text: Hmm — your heart rate just spiked to 114 bpm. That’s higher than the 98 we saw earlier. Are you feeling anxious or short of breath right now?

speaker: patient
text: A little lightheaded, yes — and my chest feels tight since I walked up the stairs before coming in.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s get you seated and take another look. We may need to adjust your anti-anginal regimen.

speaker: patient
text: I did check my blood sugar before leaving home — it was already high, around 10.2 — but I wasn’t sure if that was relevant.

speaker: doctor
text: It absolutely is. Hyperglycemia can contribute to fatigue and even transient tachycardia. Let’s recheck it now.

speaker: patient
text: That reading from earlier — the 98 bpm — matches what I measured myself at home this morning with my wrist monitor.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose is now 11.7 mmol/L — significantly higher than the 8.3 we saw earlier. Given your symptoms and this trend, we’ll add a short-term insulin correction protocol and schedule a diabetes education follow-up.
